title: How to Calculate and Communicate Extreme Event Attribution
text:
Introduction
This user guide provides step by step instructions on how to calculate and communicate Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR). These metrics are fundamental parts of Extreme Event Attribution (EEA). EEA aims to evaluate how human-induced changes in Earth’s state, like increased atmospheric greenhouse gases, affect the likelihood of extreme climate events. Along with calculations, this guide will show how these metrics can be used to visualize the shift in event probability between the factual and counterfactual world. Additionally, the tutorial will explain the significance of FAR and RR in climate science and how these metrics and visuals may be used to communicate the causality of human behaviors on the climate to decision-making stakeholders.
By utilizing these metrics, you can provide a numerical answer to the question: “How much did a specific factor increase the risk of an event?”

title: How to Calculate and Communicate Extreme Event Attribution
text:
Key Terms

Extreme Event Attribution (EEA): “EEA aims to evaluate how the likelihood of a specific extreme climate event (e.g. a major drought) has been affected by human activities, such as the increase of greenhouse gas concentrations associated with fossil fuel burning,” (Naveau, Hannart, and Ribes 2020).
Causal Inference: the mathematical and logical framework used to determine if a specific change in one variable is directly responsible for a change in another.
Factual vs Counterfactual Framework: To determine if a specific factor caused a change in an event, we compare two distinct scenarios:

Factual Scenario (\(p_1\)): The “observed” world. This scenario includes all existing conditions and the specific causal factor being studied.
Counterfactual Scenario (\(p_0\)): The “what if” world. This scenario is identical to the factual one, except the specific causal factor is removed.

(Naveau, Hannart, and Ribes 2020)
Necessary vs. Sufficient Causation: A necessary cause must be present for an effect to occur (e.g., fuel is necessary for a fire), but it does not guarantee the effect. A sufficient cause guarantees the effect will occur (e.g., a lit match in a gas-filled room), but it may not be the only way to cause that effect (Ferreira 2023).
Spatiotemporal Scale: The combination of spatial extent (geographic area) and temporal duration (time period) at which environmental phenomena occur, are studied, or are addressed by policy (“Spatiotemporal Scale → Area → Sustainability. Sustainability Directory” 2024).
Extreme Value Theory (EVT): “A statistical approach that focuses on analyzing extreme events or values in data, rather than assuming a normal or symmetric distribution. It provides a framework for understanding and modeling the behavior of extreme values that exceed a certain threshold,” (“What Is Extreme Value Theory? Certificate in Quantitiative Finance” 2026).
Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR): a metric used to estimate the proportion of an observed event’s risk that can be linked to a specific causal factor. It is mathematically expressed as \(FAR = 1 - (p_0 / p_1)\) (Naveau, Hannart, and Ribes 2020).
Risk Ratio (RR): a metric used to compare the probability of of an event occurring in two different scenarios to determine how much a specific factor has increased or decreased the likelihood of that event. It is mathematically expressed as \(RR = p_1 / p_0\) (Naveau, Hannart, and Ribes 2020).

title: How to Calculate and Communicate Extreme Event Attribution
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

Define and scope an event by selecting appropriate thresholds (\(u\)) and spatiotemporal scales for analysis.
Calculate Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR).
Interpret FAR and RR within the framework of Necessary vs. Sufficient causation.
Visualize metrics by creating univariate density plots to demonstrate how a causal factor shifts the probability distribution of an event.
Translate statistical outputs into conclusions that inform real-world decision-making.





Ferreira, Rebeka. 2023. “Necessary and Sufficient Conditions.” In How to Think for Yourself. Open Washington Pressbooks. https://openwa.pressbooks.pub/howtothinkforyourself/chapter/%c2%a72-necessary-and-sufficient-conditions/.


Naveau, Philippe, Alexis Hannart, and Aurélien Ribes. 2020. “Statistical Methods for Extreme Event Attribution in Climate Science.” Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application 7 (January): 89–110. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-statistics-031219-041314.


“Spatiotemporal Scale → Area → Sustainability. Sustainability Directory.” 2024. 2024. https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/spatiotemporal-scale/.


“What Is Extreme Value Theory? Certificate in Quantitiative Finance.” 2026. 2026. https://www.cqf.com/blog/quant-finance-101/what-is-extreme-value-theory.

title: Preparation & Prerequisites
text:
Knowledge Assumptions
To successfully use this guide, ensure you meet the following requirements.
You should have a good understanding of the following concepts and be able to apply them:

title: Preparation & Prerequisites
text:
Basic Probability: Understanding the formula \(p = P(X > u)\), where \(p\) represents the probability of a random variable \(X\) exceeding a specific threshold \(u\).
Distribution Functions: Familiarity with how data is distributed and how to identify different distribution models.
Climate Risk: General awareness of recent developments and findings in climate change risks.

title: Preparation & Prerequisites
text:
Data Requirements
When working with extreme events, your choice of statistical model is critical and depends heavily on the event of interest:

Avoid Normal Distributions: Standard normal or Gaussian distributions are often unsuitable for extreme events. However, a normal distribution may be applicable when studying certain events, such as mean seasonal temperatures, as they are generally “light-tailed” variables.
Prioritize Extreme Value Theory (EVT): Use EVT for analysis because it is designed to handle heavy-tailed data and values that exceed a specific threshold.







WarningData Consistency Notice



Effective use of Extreme Event Attribution requires that you have data for the same random variable (\(X\)) collected or modeled across two distinct scenarios: the factual world and the counterfactual world. The next section contains a tip on how one might gather counterfactual data.

title: 1  Defining the Event
text:
1.1 Selecting an Event
Before applying any statistical models, you must first identify the specific extreme event you wish to study. This selection should be driven by both the available data and the specific question you want to answer for your stakeholders.

title: 1  Defining the Event
text:
Identify the Hazard: Choose a clear physical phenomenon, such as a heatwave, flood, or drought, that has a measurable impact.
Determine Stakeholder Needs: Select an event that is relevant to the decision-makers you are advising, such as an event that caused significant economic damage or impacted public safety.
Verify Data Availability: Ensure that historical records or climate models exist for this specific event type in both the factual and counterfactual scenarios.







NoteWhere does Counterfactual Data come from?



Because the counterfactual world is a “what if” scenario, its data cannot be “real” in the sense of being currently observed. For climate events, it is generally obtained in one of two ways:

Climate Model Simulations: Running complex computer models (GCMs) that simulate the Earth’s climate without human-induced greenhouse gas emissions.
Historical Proxies: Using high-quality observational records from the pre-industrial era (e.g., the late 1800s) to characterize a world before significant human influence.

title: 1  Defining the Event
text:
1.2 Threshold Selection (\(u\))
Once you have selected the type of event, you must set a benchmark for what qualifies as a “rare” occurrence of that event:

Identify the Benchmark: Choose a value (\(u\)) such that any observation \(X > u\) is considered an extreme occurrence. (e.g., Event of interest: rainfall in North America over 4 hours, Threshold: \(>\) 10 inches of rain)
Balance Your Data: Avoid setting \(u\) so high that you lack enough data points for statistical validity, but keep it high enough to remain in the “tail” of the distribution.












(a) Too Low: Not Extreme











(b) Just Right: Statistical Balance











(c) Too High: Not Enough Data







Figure 1.1: Threshold selection: Too Low (Left), Just Right (Middle), Too High (Right)

title: 1  Defining the Event
text:
1.3 Boundaries
Define the specific limits of the event to ensure your analysis is contained and reproducible.

Temporal Duration: Define the time window for the event (e.g., a 24-hour peak vs. a 3-month seasonal average).
Spatial Extent: Determine the geographic boundaries, such as a specific postal code, a watershed, or a continental region.
Maintain Consistency: Apply these exact boundaries to both your factual and counterfactual datasets to ensure a valid comparison.







TipExample



Below is an example of a defined event.

Event Type: Extreme Heat Temperatures
Threshold (\(u\)): \(u > 25\degree C\) ; i.e., extreme heat events above \(25\degree C\) are considered “rare”
Boundaries:

Spatial: A localized region (e.g., “Region A”) representing a specific urban or agricultural area.
Temporal: The summer season (June–August).


We will follow this example throughout the rest of the user guide.

title: 2  Probability Estimation
text:
2.1 Select an Estimation Method
Once you have defined your event and threshold, you must estimate the probabilities of that event occurring in both the factual (\(p_1\)) and counterfactual (\(p_0\)) scenarios.
Choose a statistical method to calculate the probabilities based on your data type and the rarity of the event:

title: 2  Probability Estimation
text:
Empirical Estimation: Use this for large datasets where you can simply count the number of times the variable \(X\) exceeds your threshold \(u\).
Parametric Modeling: Fit a specific distribution (such as the Generalized Pareto Distribution used in EVT) to your data to estimate probabilities for extremely rare events that may not appear frequently in your historical record.
Climate Model Ensembles: If using modeled data, calculate the frequency of the event across multiple simulations to derive \(p_1\) and \(p_0\).

title: 2  Probability Estimation
text:
2.2 Estimate \(p_1\) and \(p_0\)
Apply your chosen estimation method to both datasets:

Calculate \(p_1\): Determine the probability of the event occurring in the factual scenario (the world with the causal factor present).
Calculate \(p_0\): Determine the probability of the same event occurring in the counterfactual scenario (the world without the causal factor).







TipExample



To calculate the probabilities, we count how many times our variable (temperature) crossed the threshold (\(u = 25\)) and divide by the total number of observations (\(n\)). In R, we can do this efficiently using the mean() function on a logical statement.

# 1. Generate Synthetic Data (The "Worlds")
set.seed(349)
counterfactual_data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 20, sd = 2) # Cooler "Natural" World
factual_data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 22, sd = 2) # Warmer "Current" World
threshold <- 25

# 2. Define the threshold
u <- 25

# 3. Calculate Probabilities
p1 <- mean(factual_data >= u)
p0 <- mean(counterfactual_data >= u)

# Display results
cat("Factual Probability (p1):", p1, "\n")

Factual Probability (p1): 0.055 

cat("Counterfactual Probability (p0):", p0)

Counterfactual Probability (p0): 0.003


Interpretation: 

Factual Probability (\(p_1\)) : There is a 5.5% chance of an event exceeding 25°C in our current, factual world (which is affected by the causal factor of human influence).
Counterfactual Probability (\(p_0\)): There is only a 0.3% chance of this event occurring in the natural, counterfactual world.









WarningSample Size Requirements



Ensure your datasets are large enough to provide statistical validity. Small sample sizes for rare events can lead to high uncertainty and wide confidence intervals, which may make your final attribution metrics unreliable.

title: 3  Calculating FAR & RR
text:
3.1 Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR)
After estimating your probabilities (\(p_1\) and \(p_0\)), you can now calculate the two primary metrics used to quantify the human influence on an extreme event.
The FAR estimates the proportion of the observed event’s risk that is linked to the causal factor being studied.
\[FAR = 1 - \frac{p_0}{p_1}\]

title: 3  Calculating FAR & RR
text:
Use the following equation to calculate the FAR:



Interpret the Output:

A FAR of 0.90 suggests that 90% of the risk of the event was attributable to the causal factor.
If \(p_1\) is much larger than \(p_0\), the FAR will approach 1, indicating a high level of attribution.

title: 3  Calculating FAR & RR
text:
3.2 Risk Ratio (RR)
The RR, also known as the “likelihood ratio,” compares the probability of an event occurring in two different scenarios to determine how much a specific factor increased or decreased the likelihood of that event.

Use the following equation to calculate the RR:

\[RR = \frac{p_1}{p_0}\]

Interpret the Output:

A RR \(=\) 2.0 means the event is twice as likely to occur in the factual world compared to the counterfactual world.
A RR \(=\) 1.0 indicates that the causal factor had no impact on the likelihood of the event.
A RR \(\le\) 1.0 indicates the causal factor decreased the likelihood of the event.








TipExample



Continuing with our synthetic 25°C threshold data, we can calculate these metrics in R using the variables we defined in the Estimation step.

# 1. Calculate Risk Ratio
risk_ratio <- p1 / p0

# 2. Calculate Fraction of Attributable Risk
far <- 1 - (p0 / p1)

# Display results
cat("Risk Ratio (RR):", round(risk_ratio, 2), "\n")

Risk Ratio (RR): 18.33 

cat("Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR):", round(far, 2))

Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR): 0.95


Interpretation: 

RR: The causal factor has made an extreme heat event above 25°C 18.3333333 times more likely to occur compared to the natural world.
FAR: We can conclude that 95% of the risk of this heat event was contributed by the causal factor, while only 5% was due to natural internal variability.

title: 4  Applying Causal Logic
text:
4.1 Probability of Necessary Causation (PN)
Calculating metrics is only the first step, as you must also interpret what those numbers mean in the context of causality. We use two primary concepts to determine the nature of a cause: Necessity and Sufficiency.
The Probability of Necessary Causation asks: Would the event have happened anyway if the causal factor were absent?

title: 4  Applying Causal Logic
text:
Metric Link: In this framework, the FAR is mathematically equivalent to the PN.
Interpretation: If your FAR is 0.9, there is a 90% probability that the extreme event would not have occurred without the human-induced factor.

title: 4  Applying Causal Logic
text:
4.2 Probability of Sufficient Causation (PS)
The Probability of Sufficient Causation asks: Is the causal factor alone enough to guarantee that the event will occur?.

Metric Link: While RR tells you how much the risk increased, the PS measures if the factor is powerful enough to produce the effect every time it is present. The formula for PS is
\[PS = 1 - \frac{1 - p_1}{1 - p_0}\]
Interpretation: In most climate studies, \(p_1\) is very small, which often results in a low PS even if the RR is very high. This indicates that while human influence made the event much more likely, it was not “sufficient” to cause it on its own.







TipExample



To understand how to apply this logic to the metrics calculated in the previous section:



Table 4.1: Summary of Attribution Metrics










Metric
Variable
Value




Factual Probability
\(p_1\)
0.055


Counterfactual Probability
\(p_0\)
0.003


Risk Ratio
RR
18.33


Fraction of Attributable Risk
FAR
0.95







Evaluate Necessity (PN)
Because the FAR is 0.95, the Probability of Necessary Causation is 95%.

Interpretation: There is a 95% likelihood that an extreme heat event \(> 25\degree C\) would not have occurred without the influence of human-induced climate change. The causal factor was a “necessary” trigger for the event to reach this magnitude.



Evaluate Sufficiency (PS)
Using the probabilities above, we calculate the Probability of Sufficient Causation:

Calculation: \(PS = 1 - \frac{1 - 0.055}{1 - 0.003} = 1 - \frac{0.945}{0.997} \approx 0.0521565\)
Interpretation: The PS is only 5.2%. This means that while human influence made an extreme heat event \(> 25\degree C\) much more likely, it was not “sufficient” to guarantee the event on its own; other natural factors still had to align.

title: 5  Visualizing & Communicating Results
text:
5.1 Creating Density Plots
After calculating your metrics and applying causal logic, you should visualize the data to demonstrate the shift in probabilities between factual and counterfactual worlds, along with translating your statistical findings for stakeholders. In the application below, we will represent counterfactual and factual worlds with probability density functions (PDF) and visualize the PDF with a Univariate Density Plot.
Here is a great resource if you need a refresher on Probability Density Functions (Frost 2022).
Why use PDFs and Univariate Density Plots?

title: 5  Visualizing & Communicating Results
text:
5.1.1 Univariate Density Plot
Use a density plot to provide a visual that shows how the causal factor shifts the distribution of the event.

Plot the Distributions: Generate two overlapping density curves—one for the factual world and one for the counterfactual world.
Mark the Threshold (\(u\)): Add a vertical line at your chosen threshold to clearly define the “extreme” zone.
Highlight the Probability Shift: Use shading or arrows to show the increase in area under the curve beyond the threshold in the factual world.







TipExample: Univariate Plot




library(ggplot2)

# 1. Generate example data
set.seed(349)
counterfactual_data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 20, sd = 2)
factual_data <- rnorm(1000, mean = 22, sd = 2) 
threshold <- 25

# 2. Calculate probabilities (p0 and p1)
# We find the mean of a logical vector to get the proportion > threshold
p0 <- mean(counterfactual_data > threshold)
p1 <- mean(factual_data > threshold)

# 3. Combine into a dataframe for plotting
data <- data.frame(
  temp = c(counterfactual_data, factual_data),
  world = rep(c("Counterfactual (Natural)", "Factual (Observed)"), each = 1000)
)

# 4. Create the plot
ggplot(data, aes(x = temp, fill = world)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed", linewidth =  1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("blue", "red")) +
  annotate("label", x = 26.25, y = 0.2, 
           label = paste0("u = ", threshold), 
           fill = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Probability Shift of Extreme Heat Events",
    x = "Temperature (°C)",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Scenario"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







Figure 5.1: Comparison of Factual and Counterfactual Temperature Distributions





Reminder: The above plot was created using fake, generated data. However, for the sake of the example, we are still pretending that the difference in the counterfactual and factual data was caused by some human influence.



Table 5.1: Summary of Attribution Metrics










Metric
Variable
Value




Factual Probability
\(p_1\)
0.055


Counterfactual Probability
\(p_0\)
0.003


Risk Ratio
RR
18.33


Fraction of Attributable Risk
FAR
0.95










5.1.2 Interpret the Plot
The above density plot visualizes the core theory of event attribution. Use the following points to interpret the visual relationship between the factual and counterfactual worlds:

Identify the Shift: Notice that the Factual (Red) distribution has shifted to the right compared to the Counterfactual (Blue) distribution. This horizontal shift represents the influence of the causal factor (e.g., human activities) on the average climate conditions.
Locate the “Extreme” Zone: The area to the right of the dashed vertical line (\(u\)) represents the “tail” of the distribution. Any data point in this zone is considered an extreme event. We can see that there are nonzero probabilities for extreme temperatures above the threshold in both the factual and counterfactual worlds.
Compare \(p_0\) and \(p_1\):

The small counterfactual (blue shaded) area beyond the threshold represents \(p_0\) (the probability of the event occurring naturally without the causal factor).
The much larger factual (red shaded) area beyond the threshold represents \(p_1\) (the probability of the event occurring in our current observed world).

Visualize the Risk Ratio: In the plot, the Risk Ratio is represented as the ratio between the blue and red shaded areas to the right the threshold line. Visually, you can see that the red area is several times larger than the blue area which confirms the Risk Ratio of 18.33. Thus, in our example, the influence of the causal factor (i.e., human activities) has made the occurrence of extreme heat events above 25\(\degree\)C 18.33 times more likely.
Visualize the FAR: While the Risk Ratio compares the sizes of the two areas, the FAR tells you what percentage of the “Current Risk” (the red area) is “new”, or comes as a result of the causal factor. In the example, the FAR is 0.95. You can explain that 95% of the red shaded area past the threshold is “excess risk” created by the shift in distribution. Only the remaining 5% (the part overlapping with the original blue area past the threshold) represents the natural, baseline risk.







NoteCounterfactual & Factual Overlap



The area where the blue and red curves overlap represents events that could have happened in either world. However, the area under the red curve that does not overlap with the blue represents the risk specifically added by human influence.

title: 5  Visualizing & Communicating Results
text:
5.2 Communicate Metrics & Plots
When communicating with decision-makers, translate abstract metrics into a narrative that informs real-world policy or adaptation.

Use the Risk Ratio for Likelihood: Instead of saying “RR = 10,” tell stakeholders the event is “10 times more likely” due to the causal factor.
Use FAR for Responsibility: Instead of saying “FAR = 0.90”, use the Fraction of Attributable Risk to explain what proportion of the risk is directly linked to human behavior.
Clarify Causality: Explicitly state whether the factor was a “necessary” trigger, but be careful to note if it was not “sufficient” to cause the event on its own. You may need to use an additional example (such as the fuel and fire example in the Key Terms section) to explain the difference between necessary and sufficient causation.

When presenting the plot, do not assume the audience understands probability density. Use the following translation strategies:

Define the “Hill”: Explain that the “hill” represents where the variable (e.g., temperature) spends most of its time. The higher the peak, the more common that value is.
The “Shift” Narrative: Describe the movement of the factual curve as the “new normal.” Instead of saying “the mean shifted,” say “the entire climate has moved into warmer territory, pushing the whole curve with it.”
Focus on the “Tail”: Direct their eyes to the far right of the plot. Explain that while the middle of the “hill” moved a little, the “tail” (the extreme zone) grew significantly. This helps them understand why a small change in average temperature leads to a massive change in disaster frequency.

title: 6  Troubleshooting & Evaluation
text:
6.1 Common Technical Errors
Even with the right formulas, technical hurdles or “nonsensical” results can occur. Use this guide to debug your code and validate your findings.
If you encounter issues while running your R code, refer to the table below for common symptoms and solutions.

title: Appendix A — Topic
text:
For my user guide, I’m going to provide a tutorial on calculating and communicating Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR). These metrics are fundamental parts of Extreme Event Attribution (EEA). EEA aims to evalutate how anthropogenic forcings, like increased atmospheric greenhouse gases, affect the likelihood of specific extreme climate events. Along with calculations, the tutorial will include how these metrics can be used to visualize the shift in event probability between the factual and counterfactual world. Additionally, the tutorial will explain the significance of FAR and RR in climate science and how these metrics and visuals may be used to communicate the causality of human behaviors on the climate to decision-making stakeholders.






NoteComments/suggestions



Ok. Make sure you connect them appropriately to the climate risk issue, and you may want to also look at different ways to visualize climate risk - Lace Padilla has some good stuff on that.

title: Appendix B — Needs Assessment
text:
B.0.0.1 Guide Content
This user guide will cover how the metrics of Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) can be used as tools for determining the contribution of a causal factor to extreme events. More specifically, the guide will begin by giving background on the factual vs counterfactual setup where it defines two scenarios, one where a specific causal factor is present (p1) and one where it is absent (p0). It will then define the metrics of FAR and RR by giving step by step instructions for calculation RR and FAR. We will then explain how to interpret these metrics along with how to apply them to the theoretical framework of probability of necessary causation and the probability of sufficient causation. In a final application, the guide will show how to visualize the shifts in event probability between the factual and counterfactual world through the use of univariate density plots. These visuals will demonstrate how a causal factor shifts the distribution and changes the likelihood of extreme outcomes. Throughout this content, each topic/step will be explained generally and will also follow an application example for Extreme Event Attribution (as it relates to climate science) to show how these metrics translate to real-world decision-making.


B.0.0.2 User Assumptions
The primary users of this user guide are statisticians, data scientists, and risk analysts across various fields (e.g., climate science, epidemiology, or engineering) who need to quantify the influence of a specific factor on the occurrence of a rare event. The user guide assumes that readers have the following prerequisites:

Understanding of basic probabilities: p = P(X > u), where X is a random variable and u is a threshold value. Additionally, an understanding of random variables and probability distribution functions is needed.
An interest in the logic/reasoning of “necessary” vs. “sufficient” causation.
Basic knowledge of climate risk issues and themes of recent scientific findings in the last 20 years related to human effects on climate change.



B.0.0.3 User Needs
Ultimately, the user wants to provide a numerical answer to the question “How much did factor A increase the risk of event B?” where the numerical answer lies in the calculation and interpretation of FAR and RR. Someone trying to do this will first need help with understanding and following mathematical equations to calculate their metrics. After that, they will need guidance on interpreting FAR and RR through the lens of complex theoretical frameworks and how these interpretations can be communicated to stakeholders. When introducing new complex ideas, it will be important to make conscious design choices that will keep the user engaged and motivated to continue learning. Additionally, it will be important to caution users of requirements or restrictions of the data that these metrics are being applied to (e.g. sample size and distribution).
There are a couple resources that can provide a more thorough background on some of the concepts covered. The first resource is the book Causality: Models, Reasoning and Inference by Judea Pearl which teaches the foundations of the underlying causality theory in the user guide. Additionally, the annual review article that inspired this user guide, “Statistical Methods for Extreme Event Attribution in Climate Science” by Naveau et al. also serves as an in-depth reference for applying FAR and RR to complex, dynamic systems.

title: Appendix C — Task Analysis
text:
C.0.0.1 Task Prerequisites
Given that this is a user guide for calculating statistical metrics, FAR and RR, and interpreting them, the only prerequisites are knowledge ones as we will not be applying these concepts to any code. The knowledge prerequisites are:

title: Appendix C — Task Analysis
text:
C.1 Additional Guidance
Your check-in should answer these basic questions (and similar concerns that apply more directly to your topic).
Once you’ve completed the check-in, you can use this section to jump-start an introduction/set-up/getting started section in your user guide. This document should remain as an appendix to your main report.

title: 5  Visualizing & Communicating Results
text:
5.3 Answer the Question
In the introduction of this user guide, we set out to be able to answer the question: “How much did a specific factor increase the risk of an event?”. Now that we know how to calculate, visualize, and communicate statistical metrics, we can provide a concrete answer.






TipExample



Using our example of extreme heat events from above:

Question: “How much did human-induced influence (the causal factor) increase the risk of an extreme heat event exceeding 25°C?“
Results: We observed that the factual world (with human-induced warming) produced a \(p_1\) of 0.055, while the natural, counterfactual world produced a \(p_0\) of only 0.003. From \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), we calculated a Risk Ratio of 18.3 and a Fraction of Attributable Risk of 0.95.
Conclusion: The human-induced causal factor made extreme heat events \(>\) 25\(\degree\)C 18.3 times more likely to occur. Additionally, our results indicate that 95% of the risk of exceeding the 25\(\degree\)C threshold is directly attributable to the human-induced causal factor.

Note: our application of causal logic could also be added to the Conclusion for a deeper interpretation of the results.







Frost, Jim. 2022. “Probability Density Function: Definition & Uses. Statistics by Jim.” August 28, 2022. https://statisticsbyjim.com/probability/probability-density-function/.

title: 6  Troubleshooting & Evaluation
text:
Error Message
Likely Cause
Solution




Error: object 'p1' not found
Variable sequence error.
Ensure the code chunk that calculates p1 and p0 is placed above the text or table where you call them.


RR = Inf
The “Zero \(p_0\)” problem.
Your threshold \(u\) is so high that the event never occurred in the natural world. Increase your sample size (n) or slightly lower your threshold.


Warning: Removed k rows
Data out of bounds.
Your threshold \(u\) is set outside the range of your actual data points. Check your data range using summary(data).









ImportantA Note on Infinity (Inf)



In attribution science, a Risk Ratio of Inf (Infinity) occurs when \(p_0\) is 0. While this suggests the event was “impossible” without human influence, it is usually a sign that your sample size is too small to capture rare natural events. Try increasing your simulation sample size for more stable results.

title: 6  Troubleshooting & Evaluation
text:
6.2 Statistical Sanity Check
Before you publish your results, perform a “Sanity Check” to ensure the logic holds up. If your results look like the examples below, you may need to re-evaluate your model:

Is \(p_1\) smaller than \(p_0\)?: In a warming world example, the factual probability should be higher. If \(p_1 < p_0\), check if you accidentally swapped your data labels or if you are studying a variable that should be decreasing (like extreme cold).
Is the Risk Ratio (RR) exactly 1?: This means the causal factor had zero impact. While possible, double-check that your “Factual” and “Counterfactual” datasets aren’t actually identical.
Is the FAR negative?: A negative Fraction of Attributable Risk suggests the factor reduced the risk. If you are studying human-induced warming and heatwaves, a negative FAR usually indicates a calculation error in the formula \(1 - (p_0 / p_1)\).

title: 6  Troubleshooting & Evaluation
text:
6.3 The “So What?” Test
Finally, evaluate the communicative value of your result. Ask yourself:

“If I tell a policymaker that an event is 1.2 times more likely, is that a meaningful change, or is it within the noise of natural variability?”

Attribution is most powerful when the results are distinct enough to drive action. If your results are borderline, consider if your threshold \(u\) is truly capturing an “extreme” or just a “heavy” event.
